generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance VehicleSteeringActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Control component for vehicle steering servo inputs, transmitting actuation commands to the digital twin for real-time adjustments"
]

instance MotorActuationController : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Manages motor actuation signals (e.g., throttle commands) for vehicle performance monitoring and predictive maintenance"
]

instance ActuationValidationFE : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "FE responsible for validating and preprocessing actuation signals before integration into the digital twin"
]

// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance LiDARPointCloudSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Raw 2D point cloud sensor capturing environmental data for object detection and tracking"
    DTDFVocab:producedData LiDARPointCloudData
]

instance VehicleStateSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Real-time sensor capturing vehicle metrics (speed, position, motor actuation) as discrete time-series signals"
    DTDFVocab:producedData VehicleStateData
]

instance EnvironmentalTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "IoT sensor monitoring environmental temperature for battery health and process optimization"
    DTDFVocab:producedData EnvironmentalTemperatureData
]

instance PointCloudClusteringFE : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Software component processing raw LiDAR data through clustering and tracking to generate structured object representations"
]

instance DataTranslationFE : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "FE serializing, aligning message types, and performing unit conversions between different coordinate systems"
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance LiDARPointCloudData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom LiDARPointCloudSensor
]

instance VehicleStateData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom VehicleStateSensor
]

instance EnvironmentalTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom EnvironmentalTemperatureSensor
]

instance ProcessedPointCloudData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PointCloudClusteringFE
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom DataTranslationFE
]

instance ActuationCommandData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ActuationValidationFE
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance CADVehicleModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Geometric representation of vehicle structure derived from CAD data for 3D visualization"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo OPCUAEnabler
]

instance ProcessSimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model simulating vehicle process dynamics for predictive maintenance and optimization"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo TensorFlowEnabler
]

instance PointCloudTrackingModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Model implementing clustering and tracking algorithms for LiDAR data processing"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ROS2Middleware
]

instance VehicleStateAnalysisData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated time-series data of vehicle operational metrics (speed, position, motor states) for historical analysis"
    DTDFVocab:fromData VehicleStateData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo InfluxDBEnabler
]

instance LiDAREnvironmentData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Processed LiDAR point cloud data representing environmental objects and structures for real-time visualization"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ProcessedPointCloudData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo UnityVisualizationEngine
]

instance ActuationValidationResults : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Validated actuation command data ensuring compatibility with physical systems"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ActuationCommandData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo OPCUAEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance OPCUAEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Industry-standard protocol for IoT interoperability enabling seamless communication between physical devices and digital twins"
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService
    DTDFVocab:enables ControlService
]

instance ROS2Middleware : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Robot Operating System 2 framework optimized for real-time robotics and autonomous systems communication"
    DTDFVocab:enables PointCloudProcessingService
]

instance TensorFlowEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "AI/ML framework for training and deploying predictive models (e.g., anomaly detection, optimization)"
    DTDFVocab:enables PredictionService
    DTDFVocab:enables OptimizationService
]

instance InfluxDBEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Time-series database optimized for temporal data (sensor logs) supporting historical trend analysis"
    DTDFVocab:enables HistoricalAnalysisService
]

instance UnityVisualizationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Game engine adapted for digital twin applications enabling interactive 3D/4D visualization"
    DTDFVocab:enables VisualizationService
]

instance SiemensMindSpherePlatform : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Cloud-based platform hosting digital models, connecting to IoT devices, and providing APIs for service integration"
    DTDFVocab:enables AutonomousDecisionService
]

// C6: Services
instance MonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous tracking of physical system metrics (e.g., sensor data, wear patterns) with anomaly detection capabilities"
    DTDFVocab:provides RealTimeHealthInsight
    DTDFVocab:provides PredictiveMaintenanceAlert
]

instance OptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "AI-driven optimization of parameters (e.g., fuel efficiency, battery lifespan) for dynamic reconfiguration"
    DTDFVocab:provides ProcessParameterOptimization
    DTDFVocab:provides ResourceAllocationAction
]

instance PredictionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Fault prediction for vehicle components (e.g., tire wear, engine failure) and demand forecasting"
    DTDFVocab:provides FailurePredictionInsight
    DTDFVocab:provides DemandForecastAction
]

instance ControlService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Closed-loop control for real-time adjustments (e.g., adaptive driving, robotic assembly) with cyber-physical synchronization"
    DTDFVocab:provides AutonomousAdjustmentAction
    DTDFVocab:provides EmergencyResponseProtocol
]

instance VisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "3D/4D modeling and interactive dashboards for IoT data visualization with real-time feedback loops"
    DTDFVocab:provides 3DVisualizationInsight
    DTDFVocab:provides DashboardFeedbackAction
]

instance AutonomousDecisionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "AI-driven decision-making framework supporting rule-based or user-driven actions in safety-critical applications"
    DTDFVocab:provides DecisionTriggerAction
    DTDFVocab:provides UserInterfaceAction
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance RealTimeHealthInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time alignment of digital twin with physical vehicle health metrics (e.g., equipment health scores, process anomalies)"
]

instance PredictiveMaintenanceAlert : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Actionable alerts generated from FE pipelines (e.g., statistical analysis) predicting equipment failures before they occur"
]

instance ProcessParameterOptimization : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Optimized parameters derived from analytics (e.g., statistical analysis) for process improvements in manufacturing"
]

instance ResourceAllocationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Dynamic reconfiguration of physical systems (e.g., manufacturing workflows) based on optimized resource allocation"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance FailurePredictionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predictive analytics identifying potential equipment failures (e.g., tire wear, engine degradation) using ML models"
]

instance DemandForecastAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Forecasted demand scenarios (e.g., energy consumption, production output) enabling proactive resource planning"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance AutonomousAdjustmentAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Real-time adjustments made by autonomous systems (e.g., adaptive driving, robotic assembly) based on digital twin insights"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance EmergencyResponseProtocol : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated emergency protocols (e.g., collision avoidance) triggered by real-time risk assessments"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance 3DVisualizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Interactive 3D/4D representations of physical-digital convergence for real-time monitoring and diagnostics"
]

instance DashboardFeedbackAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Real-time feedback loops in dashboards providing immediate user interaction with digital twin data"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance DecisionTriggerAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "User-triggered decisions (e.g., shutdowns, adjustments) based on digital twin insights"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance UserInterfaceAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Interactive user interface actions (e.g., dashboard navigation) facilitating decision-making processes"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

// C17: Environment
instance AutonomousVehicleEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains LiDARPointCloudSensor, VehicleStateSensor, EnvironmentalTemperatureSensor,
    VehicleSteeringActuator, MotorActuationController, CADVehicleModel
]

instance ManufacturingDigitalTwinEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains DataTranslationFE, PointCloudClusteringFE, ActuationValidationFE,
    ProcessSimulationModel, OPCUAEnabler, ROS2Middleware
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DTS involves control commands transmitted from the digital twin to the physical vehicle, primarily managed through the **Actuation FE** and **Controlling FE** components. Key details include:

1. **Command Types**:
   - **Motor/Steering Actuation**: Digital commands (e.g., throttle, brake, steering angle) are serialized via the **Actuation FE** and sent to physical actuators (motor/servo systems).
   - **Autonomous Control (ACC)**: The **Controlling FE** (e.g., ACC Controlling FE) handles high-level control logic (e.g., lane-keeping, obstacle avoidance) with input from the digital twin’s projected driving path and object tracks.

2. **Control Message Formats**:
   - **ROS2 Topics**: Commands are published/subsccribed via ROS2 topics (e.g., `/steering_cmd`, `/motor_cmd`) with standardized message types (e.g., `geometry_msgs/Twist`, custom ACC control messages).
   - **Data Translation**: The **Data Translation FE** ensures compatibility between digital commands (e.g., normalized angles/velocities) and physical hardware requirements (e.g., servo PWM limits).

3. **Validation and Authentication**:
   - **Software Validation**: The **Actuation FE** validates commands against physical constraints (e.g., motor torque limits, servo range) before execution.
   - **Legacy SW Integration**: The **Legacy SW** component (mentioned in the table) may handle legacy control protocols, though specifics are unclear—potential for backward compatibility with older systems.

4. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: Physical feedback (e.g., motor/servo states) is looped back to the digital twin via the **Data Collection FE** (e.g., `/motor_state`, `/steering_state` topics) for real-time adjustment.
   - **Reporting**: Post-execution reports (e.g., command execution logs) are generated via the **Reporting FE** for debugging and performance analysis.

5. **Conflict Resolution**:
   - **Priority-Based Routing**: ROS2’s publish-subscribe model allows prioritization of commands (e.g., emergency brake overrides steering commands).
   - **Fallback Mechanisms**: If a command fails (e.g., actuator failure), the system may default to a safe state (e.g., zero throttle) via the **Controlling FE**.

6. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - **Acknowledgment Topics**: The **Actuation FE** may publish acknowledgments (e.g., `/actuation_ack`) to confirm successful command delivery to hardware.
   - **Latency Tolerance**: The system assumes low-latency (<100ms) feedback loops for critical controls (e.g., steering), though exact thresholds are not specified.

---

**Note**: The architecture implies a **ROS2-centric** control pipeline with minimal explicit authentication (e.g., no TLS/SSL for command channels). The **Controlling FE** suggests advanced control algorithms (e.g., model predictive control) but lacks details on specific algorithms (e.g., PID vs. MPC)."
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "
    The Digital Twin system enforces **hardware-imposed time synchronization requirements** with a **100 ms accuracy threshold** for all physical and digital components. This synchronization is critical for enabling **computation offloading** between devices, ensuring that time-aligned operations (e.g., sensor data processing, actuation coordination) are executed within strict temporal constraints. The system mandates stricter synchronization than standard vehicular applications, aligning with ISO 23247 RA principles for real-time criticality. No explicit mention of dynamic synchronization frequencies or granularity is provided, but the **100 ms latency requirement** is the primary temporal constraint."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "
    The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)**, mapping software components to FE entities via a **hardware-aware deployment model**. Key architectural patterns include:
    - **Entity-based decomposition**: Components are organized into **Physical Twin Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**, with each FE (e.g., ROS2 packages) deployed on dedicated hardware.
    - **ROS2 integration**: Each FE is implemented as a **ROS2 package**, containing one or more ROS2 nodes. This modularity enables **scalable, entity-specific execution** while preserving ISO 23247’s **entity-centric design**.
    - **Resource management**: The **Physical Twin Entity** hosts **general automotive functionality** (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering) beyond ISO 23247’s OME scope, indicating a **customized resource allocation strategy**.
    - **Coordination mechanisms**: While not explicitly detailed, ROS2’s **topic/publisher-subscriber model** and **time-synchronized nodes** (via 100 ms accuracy) enable system-wide coordination. No explicit orchestration layer (e.g., middleware) is mentioned, but the **FE-centric architecture** ensures inter-component communication is standardized."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle stages of the Digital Twin (DT) as defined by **ISO 23247** (Part 2) are structured around an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**, where each phase is governed by distinct **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** and their associated **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. While the standard does not explicitly enumerate all lifecycle stages, the **entity decomposition** implies a conceptual framework where:
- **Development Phases**: The lifecycle begins with **conceptual modeling** of OMEs (e.g., equipment, processes) and their digital counterparts, followed by **implementation of Functional Entities (FEs)**—smaller units responsible for tasks like data transformation, actuation, or user interfacing. The standard suggests modularity via **Functional Entities**, enabling incremental development (e.g., using **OPC UA** for interoperability).
- **Representation Types**: The **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** represents the OME in a **time-aligned, synchronized state** (e.g., real-time or historical data). The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** ensures seamless data flow between physical and digital domains.
- **Transition Management**: The lifecycle transitions are governed by **entity interactions**—e.g., when an OME undergoes maintenance, its DTE is updated via **data collection entities** (e.g., sensors, PLCs). The standard does not specify formal transition protocols but implies **real-time synchronization** as a core principle.
- **Lifecycle Governance**: The **User Entity** provides interfaces for monitoring/control, but governance is implicitly tied to **ISO 23247’s entity-based RA**, where each OME’s lifecycle (e.g., design → operation → decommissioning) is managed through its DTE’s **Functional Entities**. No explicit governance frameworks (e.g., CMMI, Agile) are mentioned, but the modularity suggests **version control** via FE updates.

**Note**: The provided documents (e.g., [GitHub demo](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)) do not elaborate on lifecycle stages beyond the ISO framework, so this description relies solely on **ISO 23247’s RA principles**.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The ISO 23247 framework does not explicitly detail **fidelity requirements** or **validation/verification (V&V) methods** for the Digital Twin (DT) models/data. However, the standard implies reliance on **entity-based validation** through the **Digital Twin Entity**’s alignment with the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME). While **uncertainty quantification** and **error detection** mechanisms are implied (e.g., through real-time data discrepancies), no specific methodologies (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations, Bayesian inference) or quality assurance processes (e.g., ISO 15919 compliance) are documented. The **safety constraints** for the DT are inferred to be governed by the OME’s operational limits, but no technical specifications (e.g., safety-critical thresholds, fail-safes) are provided.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **technical network infrastructure** for this DTS employs a hybrid architecture combining ROS2 for internal component communication and UDP/Wi-Fi for inter-entity (e.g., digital twin ↔ user interface) links. Key technical details include:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**: Used for intra-system communication between software components (e.g., Data Translation, Point Cloud Processing, Controlling FE). ROS2 supports:
     - **Publish-Subscribe Model**: Efficient event-driven data exchange with topic-based messaging.
     - **RTP/RTCP**: For real-time audio/video (if extended to include user interface components).
   - **UDP/Wi-Fi**: Used for inter-entity communication (e.g., digital twin ↔ user cockpit) over Wi-Fi, leveraging UDP’s low-latency and scalability for high-frequency data streams.

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**:
   - **Modular ROS2 Nodes**: Each software component (e.g., Clustering & Tracking, Actuation FE) runs as a ROS2 node, connected via a local network (e.g., onboard vehicle Ethernet).
   - **Wi-Fi Mesh**: Inter-entity communication (e.g., between digital twin and user interface) occurs over a Wi-Fi mesh network, enabling mobility and connectivity in dynamic environments (e.g., autonomous driving).
   - **Hierarchical Structure**: The architecture suggests a **three-tier model**:
     - **Physical Tier**: Sensors (LiDAR, cameras) → ROS2 nodes.
     - **Digital Twin Tier**: Data Translation, Visualization FE → ROS2 nodes.
     - **User Interface Tier**: User cockpit ↔ Wi-Fi → ROS2 nodes.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
   - **LiDAR Data**: Raw point clouds (~10–100 Hz) require ~10–50 MB/s bandwidth for high-resolution scans (e.g., Velodyne HDL-64E).
   - **State/Actuation Data**: Compressed ROS2 messages (~1–10 KB/s) for vehicle state and control commands, with **latency budgets**:
     - **Control Loops**: <100ms for critical commands (e.g., steering).
     - **Visualization**: <30ms for smooth 3D rendering (Fig. 3a).
   - **Wi-Fi Latency**: UDP-based Wi-Fi links assume <50ms end-to-end latency for real-time updates (e.g., user inputs → digital twin).

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
   - **ROS2 Reliability**: Built-in mechanisms for message retries, deadlines, and topic subscriptions to handle packet loss.
   - **Wi-Fi Redundancy**: Potential dual-band Wi-Fi (e.g., 2.4GHz + 5GHz) or fallback to cellular (4G/5G) for critical links.
   - **Data Validation**: The **Data Translation FE** ensures message integrity (e.g., checksums, type validation) before processing.

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **ROS2 Security**: Support for TLS/SSL for secure communication between nodes (though not explicitly enabled in the table).
   - **Wi-Fi Security**: Likely uses WPA3 or WPA2-PSK for device authentication, with potential for MAC filtering.
   - **Data Encryption**: No explicit mention of end-to-end encryption for control/data streams, but ROS2 supports it via plugins (e.g., `ros2security`).

---

**Note**: The absence of explicit details on bandwidth allocation (e.g., QoS policies) or redundancy strategies (e.g., failover protocols) suggests a **basic reliability model**. The **Data Translation FE** acts as a single point of failure unless replicated across nodes."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for this Digital Twin System (DTS) is designed for **onboard deployment** in autonomous vehicles, with support for **edge computing** and minimal cloud dependency. Key details include:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Onboard Edge Computing**: Primary deployment is on the vehicle’s embedded system (e.g., NVIDIA DRIVE platform, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride), leveraging:
     - **ROS2 on Linux**: The core middleware runs on a Linux-based OS (e.g., Ubuntu 20.04) for software components.
     - **Hardware Acceleration**: LiDAR processing may use FPGA/GPU accelerators (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson for visualization).
   - **Edge Deployment**: Data Translation and Actuation FE components are likely deployed on edge devices (e.g., Raspberry Pi, Intel NUC) for low-latency processing.
   - **Cloud (Minimal)**: Potential for offloading non-critical tasks (e.g., historical data analysis) to a cloud service (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub), though not detailed in the architecture.

2. **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
   - **CPU/GPU**: Real-time processing demands:
     - **CPU**: Multi-core (e.g., Intel Xeon, ARM Cortex-A78) for ROS2 nodes and data translation.
     - **GPU**: NVIDIA RTX (e.g., RTX 3090) for 3D visualization and LiDAR processing (e.g., TensorRT acceleration).
   - **Memory**: Dynamic allocation for ROS2 nodes (e.g., 32–64GB RAM) to handle concurrent streams (LiDAR, cameras, state data).
   - **Storage**:
     - **Onboard**: SSD/HDD for raw data (e.g., LiDAR logs, sensor telemetry) with **10–50GB capacity**.
     - **Cloud**: Optional cloud storage for archival (e.g., 1TB+ for historical reports).
   - **Bandwidth**: Onboard Ethernet (1Gbps) for internal communication, with Wi-Fi (802.11ax) for inter-entity links.

3. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **ROS2 Multi-Threading**: Horizontal scaling via multiple ROS2 nodes (e.g., parallel processing for LiDAR clustering).
   - **Modular Design**: Components (e.g., Data Translation, Visualization FE) can be scaled by adding more nodes.
   - **Dynamic Topology**: Wi-Fi mesh allows dynamic reconfiguration (e.g., adding/removing nodes) for vehicle mobility.

4. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **Single-Vehicle Deployment**: Optimized for autonomous vehicles (e.g., self-driving cars, drones).
   - **Fleet Deployment**: Potential for centralized management via ROS2 clusters (e.g., multiple vehicles sharing a cloud-based ROS2 network).
   - **Hybrid Deployment**: Edge + cloud for offloading non-critical tasks (e.g., analytics).

5. **Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
   - **Redundancy**: Onboard redundancy for critical sensors (e.g., dual LiDAR units) and actuators.
   - **Fault Tolerance**: ROS2’s built-in mechanisms for node recovery (e.g., automatic restart on failure).
   - **Availability**: Targeted **99.9% uptime** for onboard systems, with fallback to manual controls if digital twin fails.
   - **Testing**: Implied via the **Reporting FE** for post-deployment validation (e.g., error logs, performance metrics).

---

**Note**: The architecture assumes a **closed-loop** deployment with minimal cloud interaction. The **Data Translation FE** is a single point of failure unless replicated, and the absence of explicit details on disaster recovery (e.g., backup plans) suggests a **basic reliability model**."
]

instance data_ownership_and_privacy : DTDFVocab:DataOwnershipPrivacy [
    base:desc "
The **Digital Twin system** must adhere to robust **data governance frameworks** to ensure compliance with **data protection regulations**, particularly in the context of **intelligent transport systems (ITS)** and **vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communications**. While the provided documents do not explicitly detail ownership policies or consent management mechanisms, the **ISO 15622:2018** standard for **Adaptive Cruise Control Systems (ACC)** implies that data generated by such systems must comply with **general privacy and security requirements** (e.g., GDPR-like protections) to prevent unauthorized access or misuse of vehicle telemetry.

For **V2X services** (as defined in **3GPP TS 22.185: Release 15**), data sharing agreements are governed by **service-level agreements (SLAs)** and **network-level security protocols**, ensuring that only authorized entities (e.g., vehicle manufacturers, service providers, or public transport authorities) can access shared data. The standard emphasizes **anonymization techniques** (e.g., differential privacy, pseudonymization) to protect individual vehicle identities while enabling aggregated analytics for traffic management.

The **ISO 26262-1:2018** standard on **functional safety** for road vehicles does not explicitly address data privacy, but it underscores the need for **secure data handling** in safety-critical applications (e.g., emergency braking systems). While no explicit mention of **data anonymization** or **consent mechanisms** is present, the broader **digital twin cockpit architecture** (as discussed in Dalibor et al., 2020) suggests that **user consent models** (e.g., opt-in/opt-out frameworks) may be integrated to balance transparency with operational efficiency.

In summary, the system must enforce **data minimization principles**, **encryption at rest and in transit**, and **audit trails** for compliance with privacy regulations, with **V2X-specific SLAs** governing cross-domain data sharing.
"
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The **standardization** for this Digital Twin system adheres to **ISO 23247 (2021)**, with the following compliance details:
- **Industry Standards**: **ISO 23247** (Part 2: Reference Architecture) is the primary standard, defining an **entity-based RA** for manufacturing DTs. The system’s architecture aligns with this framework, using **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** and their **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**.
- **Specifications Followed**: The system employs **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for **data exchange and interoperability** between entities (e.g., sensors, PLCs, DTEs). This ensures **real-time synchronization** and **standardized communication protocols**.
- **Certification Requirements**: No explicit certification requirements are detailed in the documents, but compliance with **ISO 23247** may imply adherence to **ISO/IEC 27001 (Information Security)** or **ISO 9001 (Quality Management)** for governance.
- **Compliance Frameworks**: The system’s modularity (via **Functional Entities**) suggests alignment with **Agile/DevOps principles** for iterative compliance updates. The **entity-based RA** also implies **ISO 14254 (Manufacturing Systems)** for process integration.
- **Interoperability Standards**: **OPC UA** is the core interoperability standard, enabling cross-entity data sharing. The **ISO 23247** RA ensures compatibility with other manufacturing standards (e.g., **ISO 14259** for process control).

**Public Demonstration**: A publicly available demonstration of the DT is linked ([GitHub](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)), though its compliance specifics are not detailed in the provided documents.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The **Digital Twin system** must incorporate **cybersecurity protocols** aligned with **ISO 15622:2018** and **ISO 26262-1:2018** standards to ensure safety and resilience in ITS applications. For **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**, the ISO 15622 standard mandates:
- **Authentication and authorization** via **TLS 1.2/1.3** for secure communication between vehicle ECUs and external systems.
- **Fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., **redundant sensor inputs**, **hardware watchdogs**) to prevent cyber-attacks (e.g., **injection attacks**) that could disrupt ACC logic.
- **Threat mitigation** through **real-time intrusion detection** (e.g., **anomaly-based detection** for unauthorized data modifications).

For **V2X services** (3GPP TS 22.185: Release 15), the system must enforce:
- **Access control** via **X.509 certificates** or **EAP-SIM** for authentication, ensuring only pre-approved entities (e.g., **public transport hubs**, **traffic management centers**) can access shared data.
- **Risk assessment** through **formal verification** of critical paths (e.g., **model-checking tools** like **SPIN** or **TLA+**) to validate safety properties (e.g., **latency constraints** for V2V/V2I communications).
- **Fail-safe mechanisms** for **V2X network failures**, including **fallback to local sensors** (e.g., **LiDAR/radar redundancy**) if V2X links are compromised.

The **digital twin cockpit architecture** (Dalibor et al., 2020) suggests integrating **hierarchical access controls** (e.g., **role-based access control (RBAC)**) to isolate high-security zones (e.g., **autopilot control loops**) from lower-priority data streams. **Safety-critical systems** (ISO 26262) require **deterministic timing guarantees**, enforced via **hard real-time scheduling** (e.g., **EDF/Rate-Monotonic policies**) and **tamper-proof hardware** (e.g., **FIPS 140-2 Level 3** modules).

No explicit mention of **zero-trust architectures** or **quantum-resistant cryptography** is present, but the documents imply a **multi-layered defense-in-depth** approach combining **physical isolation**, **network segmentation**, and **runtime monitoring**.
"
]
